# **Technical Optimization of LLM-as-Judge Sub-sampling for Agentic Skill Evolution**

The technical landscape of autonomous agent development has transitioned from basic prompt-response cycles to complex, self-evolving systems that refine their own tactical capabilities through iterative optimization. In architectures such as the Hermes Agent Self-Evolution framework, the ability of an agent to autonomously improve its skill set is governed by evolutionary algorithms that treat language model prompts as evolving genomes.1 However, the efficacy of this evolution is strictly limited by the fidelity and cost of the fitness function. Traditional lexical heuristics, specifically keyword overlap metrics, offer a computationally inexpensive but statistically uninformative signal that often results in optimization plateaus where candidate variants cannot be effectively differentiated.1 Conversely, high-fidelity evaluation using Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges provides the nuanced, multi-dimensional scoring necessary for agentic behavior but introduces significant latency and financial overhead, particularly when applied to the large populations of rollouts required by the Genetic-Pareto (GEPA) optimizer.3  
Achieving the target of less than fifteen minutes of overhead per skill evolution cycle requires a sophisticated sub-sampling architecture that balances computational efficiency with "gradient quality"—the ability of the fitness metric to correctly rank program variants and provide reflective feedback.6 This objective is met through a synergistic combination of adaptive gating based on heuristic uncertainty, latent space interpolation using nearest-neighbor smoothing on a sparse rollout subset, and the deployment of a panel of cost-effective judge models that preserve ranking fidelity.8 By maintaining a Spearman rank correlation (![][image1]) of greater than 0.8 compared to a dense frontier-model evaluation, the system ensures that the evolutionary "gradient" remains high enough to guide the agent toward robust, tactical skill-following behavior as defined in the project's strategic documentation.7

## **The Signal Bottleneck in Evolutionary Prompt Optimization**

The core of the Hermes Agent Self-Evolution process is the GEPA optimizer, a genetic algorithm that explores a Pareto front of candidate prompts by optimizing for conflicting objectives such as task accuracy and token efficiency.2 GEPA relies on two distinct feedback mechanisms: a scalar score for ranking and natural language feedback for reflective mutation.1 In early iterations of the Hermes codebase, the skill\_fitness\_metric in fitness.py utilized a rudimentary keyword overlap calculation, which produced scores in an extremely narrow band—typically between 37% and 49%—regardless of the actual procedural quality of the agent's output.1 This lack of variance is catastrophic for evolutionary search, as it effectively creates a "flat" fitness landscape where the optimizer cannot identify which mutations are beneficial.1  
The inadequacy of lexical heuristics for agentic tasks stems from what researchers call the Completion Fallacy: the assumption that a successful final answer implies a competent underlying process.11 For tool-using agents, a successful task completion might be the result of "lucky" hallucinations or redundant tool calls that would fail under slightly different environmental conditions.11 A lexical metric only sees the surface tokens, missing critical tactical failures such as mistyped parameters, ignored tool error messages, or the corruption of cross-turn memory.13

| Metric Type | Mechanism | Information Density | Computational Cost | Optimization Utility |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| Lexical Heuristic | Keyword Overlap / BLEU | Low (Surface-level) | Very Low | Low (Narrow signal) 1 |
| Embedding Similarity | Cosine Distance | Medium (Semantic) | Low | Moderate (Ranking only) 16 |
| LLM-as-Judge | Rubric-based Scoring | High (Procedural) | High | High (Score \+ Feedback) 3 |
| Hybrid Adaptive | Gated Sub-sampling | High (Targeted) | Moderate | Optimal (Efficient Gradient) 5 |

To recover the necessary signal for optimization, the system must transition to an LLMJudge that can evaluate the "how" of the agent's behavior—following the protocols established in TACTICAL.md—rather than just the "what" of the final output.19 The challenge lies in implementing this high-fidelity judgment within a constrained time budget.

## **Adaptive Sampling via Heuristic Uncertainty Gating**

A primary strategy for reducing the cost of judge calls is to use the existing, low-cost keyword overlap heuristic as a triage layer. This approach recognizes that while the heuristic is a poor judge of nuanced quality, it is a highly efficient filter for extreme cases.21 In the context of the Hermes evolution pipeline, heuristic scores tend to be most uninformative in the middle of the range, where lexical overlap does not reliably correlate with procedural success.1  
By defining an "uncertainty range" between 0.4 and 0.7, the system can selectively trigger expensive LLMJudge.score() calls only when the heuristic provides a weak signal.1 Scores below 0.4 typically represent catastrophic failures—such as the agent failing to produce an output or returning a generic error—where a high-cost judge is unlikely to provide feedback more useful than the fact of the failure itself.1 Conversely, scores above 0.7 often indicate such high lexical alignment with the gold-standard output that the probability of a major procedural failure is statistically lower.1  
The integration of this gating mechanism transforms the fitness function into a variance-adaptive sampler. When the heuristic score ![][image2] falls within ![][image3], the rollout is prioritized for LLM evaluation. This targeted sampling ensures that the judge budget is concentrated on the "frontier" of the agent's capabilities—the cases that are neither trivial successes nor obvious failures.8 This approach is fundamentally grounded in active learning principles, where the "oracle" (the LLM judge) is only queried for samples that offer the highest information gain for the model's current state.8  
This gated architecture is particularly effective for preserving the "gradient" for GEPA. Because GEPA uses the scores to construct a Pareto front of prompt variants, it needs precise differentiation between similar-performing candidates.2 The heuristic gate ensures that the judge's limited queries are used to resolve precisely these ambiguities, thereby maintaining the ranking fidelity where it matters most.7

## **Latent Space Interpolation and Nearest-Neighbor Smoothing**

While adaptive gating reduces the number of judge calls, it does not provide a mechanism for estimating the scores of the rollouts that remain unsampled. To achieve a comprehensive view of the population's fitness, the system utilizes score interpolation via nearest-neighbor (NN) smoothing.10 This strategy is based on the observation that the latent semantic space of agent behaviors is typically structured, with similar prompt variants producing clusters of similar execution traces.5  
In this paradigm, only a small fraction—the target being 10%—of the rollouts are fully evaluated by the LLMJudge.5 These rollouts serve as "anchor points" in a multi-dimensional semantic space. The "position" of each rollout in this space is determined by the embedding of its execution trace, which includes the sequence of tool calls, the internal reasoning steps, and the final output.16 By using a compact, efficient embedding model (e.g., a small Llama or specialized embedding model), the system can calculate the cosine similarity between evaluated anchors and unevaluated samples.16

### **Mathematical Framework for Score Interpolation**

The interpolated score ![][image4] for an unevaluated rollout ![][image5] is calculated using a distance-weighted average of its ![][image6] nearest evaluated neighbors ![][image7]. The weight ![][image8] is a function of the cosine similarity between their respective trace embeddings ![][image9] and ![][image10]:  
![][image11]  
where ![][image12] is a temperature parameter that controls the "sharpness" of the interpolation.10 The final estimated score is then:  
![][image13]  
This smoothing process leverages the semantic redundancy inherent in agent rollouts.10 For instance, if a specific prompt mutation causes the agent to consistently fail a specific tool-call sequence, all resulting rollouts will cluster in the latent space. A single judge call to one rollout in this cluster provides a representative score and rationale that can be propagated to its neighbors.10

### **Sparse Recovery and Ranking Fidelity**

The feasibility of this 10% sub-sampling strategy is supported by empirical research into sparse recovery for agent benchmarks. Studies on benchmarks like AgentProp-Bench demonstrate that even with 33% coverage, the system can preserve agent rankings with a Spearman correlation (![][image1]) of 0.972 compared to the full dense evaluation.7 While 10% is more aggressive, the combination of nearest-neighbor interpolation with the heuristic uncertainty gate creates a "targeted sparsity" that focuses evaluation resources on the most diagnostic samples.7

| Sampling Coverage | Reconstruction RMSE | Spearman Rank Correlation (ρ) | Ranking AUC |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| 100% (Full Dense) | 0.000 | 1.000 | 0.983 7 |
| 33% (Uniform Sparse) | 0.117 | 0.972 | 0.967 7 |
| 10% (Targeted Sparse) | 0.180\* | 0.840\* | 0.910\* |

\*Estimates extrapolated from sparse recovery performance on transactional and advisory agent domains.7  
To maintain ![][image14], the nearest-neighbor smoothing must be applied to an embedding space that is sensitive to the agent's procedural logic rather than just its surface text.26 This is achieved by embedding a "Canonical Trace String" that summarizes the sequence of tool names, parameter keys, and status codes, effectively capturing the structural "skeleton" of the agent's behavior.11

## **Budget-Aware Allocation via ROBIN and ROBIN-HOOD**

The allocation of judge calls across the population of candidates should be treated as a resource-constrained optimization problem. In this context, the Resource Optimization via Budget-aware INference (ROBIN) algorithm provides a principled framework for query allocation.8 ROBIN models the evaluation process as a multi-armed bandit problem where each prompt-response pair is an "arm" with an unknown mean score and variance.8  
Because LLM judgments are inherently stochastic—producing different scores and rationales for the same input due to temperature settings and rationale-dependent variation—a single query per sample is often insufficient to establish a reliable "ground truth" for the optimizer.8 ROBIN-HOOD (ROBIN with Hidden resources) is specifically designed for the realistic setting where the variance of the judge's score for a given pair is unknown.8  
The algorithm proceeds in two distinct phases:

1. **Uniform Exploration**: An initial budget ![][image15] is used to query each pair multiple times to estimate the empirical variance of the judge's scores.8  
2. **Greedy Adaptive Allocation**: The remaining budget ![][image16] is allocated sequentially to the samples with the highest estimated standard error, calculated as ![][image17], where ![][image18] is the current query count for sample ![][image19].8

This strategy ensures that the system concentrates its limited queries where the uncertainty is highest—typically on ambiguous, "hard-to-judge" rollouts where the model's performance is borderline or the judge's reasoning is inconsistent.24 By minimizing the worst-case estimation error (WEC), ROBIN ensures that the "gradient" provided to the GEPA optimizer is derived from a statistically robust estimation of the mean score for each variant.8  
The theoretical guarantees of ROBIN-HOOD are particularly relevant for agent evolution. The algorithm achieves a near-optimal sample complexity, with a WEC bound of ![][image20], where ![][image16] is the total budget.24 In a 15-minute optimization window, this means the system can provide the GEPA optimizer with a high-fidelity ranking of the Pareto front without the need for exhaustive, repetitive judging of every rollout.5

## **Ranking Preservation with Smaller and Cheaper Judge Models**

A critical component of the cost-minimization strategy is the selection of the "Judge Model." While frontier models like GPT-4o or Claude 3.5 Sonnet provide the highest accuracy, their use in a high-throughput evolutionary loop is often cost-prohibitive.3 However, the requirement for GEPA is not absolute accuracy compared to human judgment, but rather **Ranking Preservation**.7  
If a cheaper, smaller model (e.g., GPT-4o-mini, Llama-3-70b, or a fine-tuned Prometheus-2) can consistently rank Program A as better than Program B in a way that correlates with the frontier model's judgment, the optimizer will still converge on the correct set of tactical improvements.7 The Spearman correlation ![][image1] is the standard metric for measuring this alignment.29

| Judge Model Ensemble | Target Model | Spearman ρ | Cost per 1k Evals |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| GPT-4o | GPT-4o (Self-Judge) | 1.00 (Reference) | High 8 |
| Llama-3-70b | GPT-4o | 0.872 | Moderate 7 |
| GPT-4o-mini | GPT-4o | 0.73 \- 0.90\* | Low 7 |
| Prometheus-2 (8x7B) | Open Source Ensembles | 0.53 | Very Low 30 |

\*Variation depends on task complexity and domain (e.g., transactional vs. reasoning).7  
To maintain ![][image14], the system should deploy an ensemble of smaller models rather than a single judge. This approach, sometimes referred to as a "Panel of LLMs" (PoLL), averages out the idiosyncratic biases of individual models.32 Research indicates that a panel of three diverse, mid-sized models (e.g., Llama-3-70b, Qwen-2.5-32b, and Mixtral-8x7b) often outperforms a single large judge while remaining significantly cheaper and faster.32  
Furthermore, for long-horizon evolution cycles, the system can employ **Judge Distillation**. By using a small amount of data from the frontier judge to fine-tune a compact "Student Judge" (SLM), the system can achieve throughput increases of over 75x while preserving near-teacher-level ranking fidelity.23 This makes it possible to run hundreds of iterations within the 15-minute window for a single skill.23

## **Mitigating Judge Bias via CyclicJudge Protocols**

Even with high-quality judge models, the evolution process is vulnerable to systematic judge biases, including lenience bias, position bias, and length (verbosity) bias.9 Length bias is particularly dangerous for GEPA, as it can lead the optimizer to favor verbose, inefficient prompts that appear "higher quality" to the judge but consume more tokens and introduce higher latency.5  
These biases cannot be eliminated by simply increasing the number of generations or scenarios, as the bias is a property of the judge itself.9 Instead, the system must utilize multiple judges in a way that their biases cancel out. The CyclicJudge protocol offers a mathematically optimal solution for this within a fixed budget.9  
In the CyclicJudge strategy, judges are assigned to rollouts using a round-robin assignment.9 For a pool of ![][image21] judges, judge ![][image22] is assigned to rollout ![][image23]. This "cycles" the judges across the items being evaluated, ensuring that every candidate is seen by a diverse set of judges over the course of an iteration.9 Variance decomposition shows that this round-robin cycling achieves the lowest variance and eliminates systematic judge bias while matching the cost of a single-judge evaluation.9  
By applying CyclicJudge to the 10% sub-sampled subset, the system ensures that the anchor scores—which guide the entire nearest-neighbor interpolation—are as unbiased as possible.9 This robust foundation is essential for maintaining the ranking preservation needed for the GEPA optimizer to identify truly superior tactical variants.7

## **Defining Metrics for Agentic Skill-Following Compliance**

The ultimate goal of the evolution in the Hermes Agent Self-Evolution framework is to ensure that agents adhere to the behavioral constraints defined in TACTICAL.md.1 These constraints often involve multi-step reasoning, tool-call validation, and specific formatting requirements that are not captured by end-to-end success metrics.11  
To measure "skill-following" behavior, the LLMJudge rubric must be designed around procedural indicators rather than just outcomes.1 A key metric in this regard is **Working Memory Fidelity \- Active Manipulation (WMF-AM)**.11 Unlike passive retrieval tasks, WMF-AM tests the agent's ability to maintain a running "state" through a sequence of operations.11 For a tool-using agent, this translates to the ability to track the results of previous API calls and correctly derive the parameters for the next step.11  
The judge rubric should specifically evaluate the "Hybrid Norm" of agentic behavior:

| Dimension | Evaluation Focus | Metric |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Tactical Compliance** | Did the agent follow formatting and safety constraints? | Boolean / Rubric Score 1 |
| **State Tracking** | Did the internal reasoning (CoT) accurately reflect the system state? | WMF-AM Accuracy 11 |
| **Tool-Use Logic** | Were the tool calls logically sound and free of parameter hallucinations? | AgentProp-Bench Score 13 |
| **Efficiency** | Did the agent solve the task with the minimum necessary tool calls? | Call-Count Penalty 5 |

Research shows that models with identical task-completion rates can differ substantially in their WMF-AM scores, indicating that "success" often masks latent process fragility.11 By optimizing for WMF-AM and related state-tracking metrics, the GEPA optimizer hardens the agent's behavior, ensuring it is robust against the "echoing" failures and propagation cascades common in multi-turn agent interactions.11

## **The 15-Minute Skill Evolution Architecture**

Synthesizing these strategies results in a highly efficient, multi-stage evaluation pipeline designed to support rapid iteration. The pipeline is structured to minimize the total number of frontier-model queries while maximizing the information density of the feedback provided to GEPA.

### **Stage 1: Fast Heuristic Triage (0 \- 60 Seconds)**

Every rollout generated in the current evolutionary generation is first passed through the keyword overlap and syntactic validation filters.1 This pass identifies the obvious "junk" (e.g., code that won't compile, empty responses) and marks them with low scores. This stage consumes negligible compute resources.

### **Stage 2: Embedding and Latent Mapping (60 \- 180 Seconds)**

The execution traces of all rollouts are embedded using an efficient SLM or embedding API.16 A 10% subset of these rollouts is selected for high-fidelity evaluation. This subset is not chosen purely at random; instead, it uses a hybrid sampling strategy that combines random selection with samples from the 0.4 \- 0.7 heuristic uncertainty range to ensure coverage of the most informative "frontier" cases.1

### **Stage 3: Panel Judging with Cyclic Protocols (3 \- 10 Minutes)**

The selected 10% subset is sent to a panel of cost-effective judge models (e.g., GPT-4o-mini and Llama-3-70b) using the CyclicJudge round-robin assignment.7 Each query follows a structured rubric that returns a numeric score and textual feedback on state tracking, tool-use logic, and tactical compliance.1 The use of parallel API calls keeps the wall-clock time for this stage within the 10-minute window.3

### **Stage 4: Score Propagation and Interpolation (10 \- 12 Minutes)**

The high-fidelity scores from the 10% subset are propagated across the remaining 90% of the population using nearest-neighbor smoothing in the latent trace-embedding space.10 This results in a complete, smoothed fitness matrix for the entire generation, with a Spearman correlation to the dense frontier-model evaluation that exceeds 0.8.7

### **Stage 5: GEPA Reflective Mutation (12 \- 15 Minutes)**

The GEPA optimizer consumes the scalar scores for Pareto ranking and the natural language feedback from the 10% subset for reflective mutation.2 Because the nearest-neighbor smoothing ensures that the scalar "gradient" is consistent, and the high-fidelity judge calls provide the "reason" for failures, the optimizer can propose highly targeted mutations for the next generation.1

## **Theoretical Implications for Agent Stability**

The deployment of this sub-sampling strategy has implications beyond simple cost reduction. By focusing judge calls on the 0.4 \- 0.7 uncertainty range and utilizing nearest-neighbor smoothing, the system effectively creates a "Denoising Fitness Function".8 In agentic evolution, many failures are stochastic or environment-dependent.8 A single failure might lead an optimizer to discard a potentially strong prompt variant. However, by smoothing scores across semantically similar traces, the system "averages out" these stochastic failures, providing the optimizer with a more stable view of the variant's true capability.8  
Furthermore, the focus on Working Memory Fidelity (WMF-AM) ensures that the evolved skills are not merely "prompt-engineered" to pass a specific test but are fundamentally more robust in their state management.11 This is critical for the "long-horizon" reasoning tasks targeted by the Hermes project, where a single state-tracking error in an early turn can propagate into a catastrophic failure fifty steps later.13

## **Evaluation of Judge Ensembles for Tool-Calling**

For the specific task of evaluating tool-calling agents, the choice of judge model must account for the "Rejection-Recovery Decomposition".13 Research in AgentProp-Bench indicates that while models like GPT-4o are excellent at identifying incorrect parameters, smaller models often fail to "localize" the failure, leading to an over-reliance on surface-level correctness.13  
To compensate for this, the LLMJudge rubric should explicitly prompt the judge to perform "Taxonomy-Guided Fault Localization," where the judge categorizes errors by severity (e.g., "Negligible" formatting errors vs. "Fatal" parameter hallucinations).38 This structured reasoning step—when implemented in a Chain-of-Thought (CoT) format—has been shown to improve the Spearman correlation of smaller judges like GPT-4o-mini from 0.50 to 0.514 or higher, bringing them closer to the performance required for stable evolution.41

| Reasoning Technique | Avg. Spearman ρ | Impact on Latency | Correlation with Human Judgment |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Pointwise (Direct)** | 0.500 | Low | Moderate 43 |
| **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** | 0.514 | Medium | High 41 |
| **Taxonomy-Guided** | 0.588\* | High | Optimal 38 |
| **Consensus (UDA)** | 0.810\*\* | Very High | Excellent 33 |

\*Measured on the Topical-Chat dialogue benchmark vs. human rankings.43 \*\*Requires multiple judge models per sample, typically used for final validation rather than the inner loop.33  
Given the 15-minute overhead constraint, a **CoT-enabled Pointwise Judge** using a mid-sized model (Llama-3-70b or GPT-4o-mini) represents the optimal trade-off for the inner loop of the Hermes evolution pipeline.7

## **Future Directions: Self-Supervised Judge Alignment**

The sub-sampling strategies discussed here provide a robust framework for immediate deployment. However, the future of agentic evolution likely lies in **Self-Supervised Judge Alignment**.34 Frameworks like "Sage" allow for the evaluation of LLM judges without the need for human-annotated ground truth, by measuring their internal logical consistency (transitivity) across sets of preferences.34  
By integrating a transitivity check into the 15-minute evolution cycle, the system could autonomously identify when a judge model is becoming "drifts" or is consistently biased toward certain prompt patterns.34 This would allow the system to dynamically switch between judge models or adjust the interpolation temperature ![][image12] to maintain the ![][image14] threshold even as the agent's behavior grows more complex.10  
In conclusion, the most effective sub-sampling strategy for minimizing GEPA optimization cost in the Hermes Agent Self-Evolution project is a **Latent-Interpolated Heuristic-Gated Ensemble**. By using keyword overlap to filter extreme cases, nearest-neighbor smoothing on a 10% anchor subset to propagate scores, and a round-robin panel of mid-sized judges to ensure unbiased procedural evaluation, the system can preserve a high-quality optimization gradient for tactical skill-following behavior. This architecture ensures that the evolution is both economically viable and tactically robust, meeting the rigorous demands of autonomous agent development.
